planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 4–6 inches deep in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; great camas is the tallest and showiest of the native camas species, with stately spikes of deep blue to cream flowers in late spring that were an important food source for many Native American peoples.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, organic, slightly acidic to neutral soil in a site that is wet in spring but dries out somewhat in summer; camassia naturalizes beautifully in moist meadows, pond edges, and wet woodland margins under deciduous trees. Great camas is the tallest camas species, native to wet meadows and forest openings of the Pacific Coast ranges from British Columbia to California, producing bold spikes 2–4 feet tall of star-shaped flowers in deep violet-blue to creamy white in May and June — one of the most spectacular of all spring bulbs for naturalizing in moist settings. The bulbs were a staple starchy food for many Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest; note that the poisonous Death Camas (Anticlea venenosum) resembles camas in leaf and grows in similar habitats — they are distinguished by flower color (death camas has creamy white flowers, edible camas has blue). Plant in large sweeps for maximum impact.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; common camas is historically one of the most important food plants of Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest and Great Basin, and a spectacular spring-blooming bulb for naturalized gardens.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in fall in moist, organic soil in a location that stays moist in spring but is not permanently waterlogged; camassia bulbs naturalize readily and spread slowly over time to form dense colonies. Common camas is a western North American native bulb that was historically one of the most important food plants for many Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest and Great Basin, who harvested the starchy bulbs in vast quantities and traded them widely; Meriwether Lewis and William Clark noted large fields of blue camas during their 1805–1806 expedition and remarked on its importance as a food source. The star-shaped, violet-blue to pale blue flowers are held on 12–24 inch stems in late spring and are spectacular when naturalized in masses in moist meadow gardens, under deciduous trees, or along stream edges. Goes dormant by midsummer.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer or early fall, 3–4 inches deep, before they bloom spontaneously out of the soil without any leaves — corms planted in August will often flower in September without any soil; they produce their large, strap-like leaves in spring and go dormant by summer.

directions: Plant corms in late summer 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; autumn crocus corms will sometimes flower spontaneously without any soil if left unplanted — plant promptly after purchase. Colchicum autumnale, called autumn crocus or naked ladies for the way its large, goblet-shaped, lilac-pink flowers emerge from bare ground in September–October without any foliage (the leaves appear separately in spring and then die back by summer), is one of the most reliably magical spectacles of the fall garden — a carpet of delicate crocus-like flowers appearing suddenly from empty-looking soil. All parts of the plant are toxic (it contains colchicine, used medicinally) and should not be confused with true crocuses, which are safe. Naturalize under deciduous shrubs or in lawns where it can be left undisturbed; the foliage must not be cut until it yellows naturally in late spring.

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer 8–10 weeks before first fall frost for a fall-winter-spring harvest; in zone 8–10, sow in September for a winter harvest of the tender inner shoots that are the prized Roman 'puntarelle' salad vegetable; the plant survives light freezes and is best treated as a cool-season crop.

directions: Direct sow in late summer in rich, well-drained soil; thin to 12–18 inches once established. Puntarelle is the Italian Catalogna-type chicory grown for its distinctive hollow, asparagus-like inner shoots that emerge from the crown in fall and winter and are the centerpiece of the classic Roman winter salad — thin strips of the shoots are soaked in cold water to curl them, then tossed with a pungent anchovy, garlic, and olive oil dressing in one of Rome's most iconic dishes. The outer leaves are cooked like bitter greens or braised, while the inner shoots and their hollow stems (the puntarelle themselves) are harvested by cutting just above the root crown. In warm-climate gardens (zones 8–11), puntarelle is an outstanding cool-season crop that fills the winter vegetable gap when few other greens are available.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in moist potting mix; established plants can go into the garden in zone 9b–11 after all frost risk has passed, or grow year-round in containers that can be brought indoors in winter in zones 9a and below.

directions: Root stem cuttings easily in moist potting mix in spring; established plants prefer well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun and are drought-tolerant once established. Vicks Plant is a succulent, aromatic tropical Plectranthus with rounded, thick, velvety, grey-green leaves that release a strong, unmistakable mentholated camphor scent when touched or bruised — the exact aroma of Vicks VapoRub, from which it takes its common name. It is grown primarily as an aromatic novelty and sensory herb for children's gardens, therapy gardens, and container collections, but has traditional use as a steam inhalant for colds and congestion in South African folk medicine. The stems are succulent and snap easily in frost; treat as a houseplant or tender annual north of zone 10, or grow in containers that overwinter indoors. Propagates extremely easily from stem cuttings placed in water or moist soil.
